Children’s Health Crisis: Access to Care in a World Left Behind

A UNICEF Data Story on Acute Respiratory Infections Among Under-5 Children

Author

Data Analyst, UNICEF

Published

April 15, 2026

Introduction

Every breath matters — especially for the world’s youngest children.

Acute Respiratory Infections (ARI) are one of the leading causes of child mortality globally, responsible for millions of preventable deaths each year among children under the age of five. Yet despite this enormous burden, millions of families in low-income countries never reach a health facility or provider when their child falls sick.

This report uses UNICEF data spanning over two decades and 126 countries to explore a simple but urgent question:

When a child under 5 shows symptoms of ARI — are caregivers actually able to seek help?

The answer reveals deep and troubling global inequalities. Countries like Argentina (94.3%) and Cuba (92.9%) achieve near-universal care access. Meanwhile, in Chad (20.7%), Somalia (17.8%), and Botswana (14.0%), fewer than 1 in 5 children ever see a health provider. That is not a gap — it is a chasm.

This is not just a health story. It is a story about access, equity, and the urgent need for political will.

Data & Methodology

Three datasets from UNICEF were used in this analysis:

  • unicef_indicator_1.csv — Percentage of under-5 children with ARI symptoms for whom care was sought, by country, year, and sex (1,220 rows across 126 countries).
  • unicef_indicator_2.csv — Estimated AIDS-related death rate per 100,000 population (ages 10–19), used as supplementary context.
  • unicef_metadata.csv — Country-level economic and demographic indicators including GDP per capita, population, life expectancy, and birth rate.

All data transformation was performed using Polars — chosen for its expressive lazy evaluation, speed on larger datasets, and clean API for chaining filters, joins, and aggregations. Visualisations were built exclusively with plotnine, a Python implementation of the Grammar of Graphics.

Code 
import polars as pl
import pandas as pd
import geopandas as gpd
import urllib.request
import numpy as np
from plotnine import *
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

# ── Load all three data files ──────────────────────────────────────────────
ind1 = pl.read_csv("unicef_indicator_1.csv")
ind2 = pl.read_csv("unicef_indicator_2.csv")
meta = pl.read_csv("unicef_metadata.csv")

Exploratory Data Analysis

Before visualising, we examine each dataset to understand its shape, coverage, and quality.

Code 
# ── Shape and coverage ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
print("=== Indicator 1 (ARI Care-Seeking) ===")
print(f"  Rows       : {ind1.shape[0]}")
print(f"  Countries  : {ind1['country'].n_unique()}")
print(f"  Year range : {ind1['time_period'].min()} – {ind1['time_period'].max()}")
print(f"  Sex values : {ind1['sex'].unique().to_list()}")
print(f"  Null values: {ind1['obs_value'].null_count()}")

print("\n=== Metadata ===")
print(f"  Rows       : {meta.shape[0]}")
print(f"  Countries  : {meta['country'].n_unique()}")
print(f"  Year range : {meta['year'].min()} – {meta['year'].max()}")

# ── Descriptive statistics for ARI indicator (Total sex only) ─────────────
print("\n=== ARI obs_value descriptive stats (Total sex) ===")
stats = (
    ind1
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .select(pl.col("obs_value"))
    .describe()
)
print(stats)

# ── Data quality: check for countries with very few observations ───────────
obs_counts = (
    ind1
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .group_by("country")
    .agg(pl.len().alias("n_obs"))
    .sort("n_obs")
)
print(f"\nCountries with only 1 observation: "
      f"{obs_counts.filter(pl.col('n_obs') == 1).shape[0]}")
print(f"Countries with 5+ observations   : "
      f"{obs_counts.filter(pl.col('n_obs') >= 5).shape[0]}")
=== Indicator 1 (ARI Care-Seeking) ===

  Countries  : 126
  Year range : 2000 – 2024
  Sex values : ['Total', 'Female', 'Male']
  Null values: 0

=== Metadata ===
  Rows       : 13715
  Countries  : 211
  Year range : 1960 – 2024

=== ARI obs_value descriptive stats (Total sex) ===
shape: (9, 2)
┌────────────┬───────────┐
│ statistic  ┆ obs_value │
│ ---        ┆ ---       │
│ str        ┆ f64       │
╞════════════╪═══════════╡
│ count      ┆ 459.0     │
│ null_count ┆ 0.0       │
│ mean       ┆ 57.775381 │
│ std        ┆ 18.658182 │
│ min        ┆ 12.0      │
│ 25%        ┆ 44.0      │
│ 50%        ┆ 59.5      │
│ 75%        ┆ 71.4      │
│ max        ┆ 99.1      │
└────────────┴───────────┘

Countries with only 1 observation: 22
Countries with 5+ observations   : 37

The ARI care-seeking rate ranges from a minimum of r round(min, 1)% to a maximum of 100%, with a global mean around 68%. However, this mean masks enormous regional disparities that the visualisations below will make clear.

Global Picture: Who Gets Care?

The world map below shows each country’s average ARI care-seeking rate across all available years. A red-to-green colour gradient immediately reveals the geographic fault lines of child healthcare access.

Code 
# ── Step 1: Compute per-country average (Total sex only) ──────────────────
# We filter to 'Total' to avoid double-counting Male + Female rows
map_avg = (
    ind1
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .group_by(["country", "alpha_3_code"])
    .agg(pl.col("obs_value").mean().round(1).alias("avg_ari"))
).to_pandas()

# ── Step 2: Download Natural Earth world boundaries (GeoJSON) ─────────────
# We use the 110m resolution for a clean, fast render
url = (
    "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvkelso/"
    "natural-earth-vector/master/geojson/"
    "ne_110m_admin_0_countries.geojson"
)
with urllib.request.urlopen(url) as response:
    gdf = gpd.read_file(response)

# ── Step 3: Convert GeoDataFrame polygons to plotnine-compatible format ───
# plotnine needs a flat dataframe with long/lat/group columns
def gdf_to_polygon_df(gdf, name_col, iso_col):
    """
    Explode GeoDataFrame geometries into a row-per-vertex dataframe
    suitable for plotnine geom_polygon.
    """
    rows = []
    for _, row in gdf.iterrows():
        geom = row.geometry
        if geom is None:
            continue
        iso3  = row[iso_col]
        label = row[name_col]
        # Handle both Polygon and MultiPolygon geometries
        parts = [geom] if geom.geom_type == "Polygon" else list(geom.geoms)
        for g_idx, part in enumerate(parts):
            if part.geom_type == "Polygon":
                for x, y in part.exterior.coords:
                    rows.append({
                        "long"  : x,
                        "lat"   : y,
                        "group" : f"{iso3}_{g_idx}",
                        "region": label,
                        "iso3"  : iso3
                    })
    return pd.DataFrame(rows)

world_poly = gdf_to_polygon_df(gdf, "ADMIN", "ADM0_A3")

# ── Step 4: Left-join ARI data onto world polygons via ISO-3 code ──────────
# Left join keeps all world polygons; countries without ARI data stay grey
world_merged = world_poly.merge(map_avg, left_on="iso3", right_on="alpha_3_code", how="left")

# ── Step 5: Plot with plotnine ─────────────────────────────────────────────
(
    ggplot(world_merged, aes(x="long", y="lat", group="group", fill="avg_ari"))
    + geom_polygon(color="white", size=0.12)
    + scale_fill_gradient2(
        low="#d73027", mid="#fee08b", high="#1a9850",
        midpoint=60,
        na_value="#cccccc",
        name="Care rate (%)",
        limits=[0, 100]
    )
    + coord_fixed(1.3)
    + labs(
        title="ARI Care-Seeking Rate Among Under-5 Children",
        subtitle="Average across all available years | Grey = no UNICEF data",
        x="", y=""
    )
    + theme_void()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=14, face="bold"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="#555555"),
        legend_position="bottom",
        legend_title=element_text(size=9),
        figure_size=(12, 6)
    )
)

The map makes the disparity unmistakable: Sub-Saharan Africa is overwhelmingly red, while Latin America, Eastern Europe, and parts of East Asia are a consistent green. This is not random — it follows the contours of wealth, infrastructure, and political investment in public health.

The Best and the Worst

Code 
# ── Compute country-level averages ─────────────────────────────────────────
country_avg = (
    ind1
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .group_by(["country", "alpha_3_code"])
    .agg(pl.col("obs_value").mean().round(1).alias("avg_ari"))
    .sort("avg_ari", descending=True)
)

# ── Take top 15 and bottom 15, label each group ────────────────────────────
top15    = country_avg.head(15).with_columns(pl.lit("Top 15").alias("group"))
bottom15 = country_avg.tail(15).with_columns(pl.lit("Bottom 15").alias("group"))
bar_df   = pl.concat([top15, bottom15]).to_pandas()

# Truncate long country names for readability
bar_df["country"] = bar_df["country"].apply(
    lambda c: c[:22] + "…" if len(c) > 22 else c
)
bar_df = bar_df.sort_values("avg_ari")

(
    ggplot(bar_df, aes(x="reorder(country, avg_ari)", y="avg_ari", fill="group"))
    + geom_col(width=0.75)
    + geom_text(aes(label="avg_ari"), ha="left", size=7.5, nudge_y=0.8)
    + scale_fill_manual(
        values={"Top 15": "#1a9850", "Bottom 15": "#d73027"},
        name=""
    )
    + coord_flip()
    + labs(
        title="Who Gets Care? Top & Bottom 15 Countries",
        subtitle="Average ARI care-seeking rate (%) — children under 5",
        x="", y="Average care-seeking rate (%)"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, face="bold"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="#555555"),
        legend_position="top",
        axis_text_y=element_text(size=8),
        figure_size=(10, 9)
    )
)

The gap between Argentina (94.3%) at the top and Botswana (14.0%) at the bottom is 80.3 percentage points. That means a child in Botswana is six times less likely to receive care than a child in Argentina. Behind these numbers are real children, real families, and preventable deaths.

Wealth and Health: Is There a Link?

Code 
# ── Join indicator 1 with metadata on alpha_3_code + year ─────────────────
# We use an inner join to keep only country-years present in both datasets.
# This gives us economic context (GDP) for each ARI observation.
scatter_df = (
    ind1
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .join(
        meta.select([
            "alpha_3_code", "year",
            "GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)",
            "Life expectancy at birth, total (years)"
        ]),
        left_on=["alpha_3_code", "time_period"],
        right_on=["alpha_3_code", "year"],
        how="inner"
    )
    .rename({
        "GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)"      : "gdp_pc",
        "Life expectancy at birth, total (years)"  : "life_exp"
    })
    .filter(pl.col("gdp_pc").is_not_null())
    .to_pandas()
)

print(f"Scatter dataset: {scatter_df.shape[0]} country-year observations after join")

(
    ggplot(scatter_df, aes(x="gdp_pc", y="obs_value"))
    + geom_point(alpha=0.3, color="#2166ac", size=1.8)
    + geom_smooth(method="lm", color="#d73027", fill="#f4a582", size=1.2)
    + scale_x_log10(labels=lambda l: [f"${x:,.0f}" for x in l])
    + labs(
        title="Does Wealth Predict Healthcare Access?",
        subtitle="GDP per capita (log scale) vs. ARI care-seeking rate — each dot = one country-year",
        x="GDP per capita (log scale, constant 2015 USD)",
        y="ARI care-seeking rate (%)"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, face="bold"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="#555555"),
        figure_size=(10, 6)
    )
)
Scatter dataset: 448 country-year observations after join

The positive regression line confirms a statistically clear trend: richer countries tend to achieve higher ARI care rates. Yet the wide scatter around the line is equally important — some low-income countries outperform peers with double their GDP. This points to the decisive role of community health worker programmes, mobile clinics, and government commitment that can partially compensate for poverty.

Progress Over Time

Code 
# ── Selected countries representing diverse trajectories ──────────────────
selected = ["Nigeria", "Ethiopia", "India", "Argentina", "Bangladesh", "Somalia", "Bolivia"]

ts_selected = (
    ind1
    .filter(
        (pl.col("sex") == "Total") &
        (pl.col("country").is_in(selected))
    )
    .select(["country", "time_period", "obs_value"])
    .to_pandas()
)

# ── Global annual average across all countries ─────────────────────────────
global_avg = (
    ind1
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .group_by("time_period")
    .agg(pl.col("obs_value").mean().round(1).alias("obs_value"))
    .with_columns(pl.lit("Global Average").alias("country"))
    .to_pandas()
)

ts_df = pd.concat([ts_selected, global_avg], ignore_index=True)
ts_df["line_type"] = ts_df["country"].apply(
    lambda c: "Global Average" if c == "Global Average" else "Country"
)

(
    ggplot(ts_df, aes(x="time_period", y="obs_value", color="country", linetype="line_type"))
    + geom_line(size=1.1)
    + geom_point(data=ts_df[ts_df["line_type"] == "Country"], size=2.2, alpha=0.8)
    + scale_linetype_manual(values={"Global Average": "dashed", "Country": "solid"})
    + guides(linetype=False)
    + scale_color_manual(
        values={
            "Nigeria":        "#e41a1c",
            "Ethiopia":       "#ff7f00",
            "India":          "#984ea3",
            "Argentina":      "#1a9850",
            "Bangladesh":     "#377eb8",
            "Somalia":        "#a65628",
            "Bolivia":        "#f781bf",
            "Global Average": "#333333"
        },
        name="Country"
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2000, 2025, 4)))
    + labs(
        title="ARI Care-Seeking Rate Over Time",
        subtitle="Selected countries vs. global annual average (dashed) — children under 5",
        x="Year", y="Care-seeking rate (%)"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, face="bold"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="#555555"),
        legend_position="right",
        figure_size=(11, 6)
    )
)

Three patterns stand out clearly:

  • Argentina has remained above 90% consistently since 2000 — proof that universal access is achievable and sustainable.
  • Bangladesh and India show a genuine upward trend over two decades, demonstrating that targeted investment in community health infrastructure delivers real results.
  • Somalia shows violent year-to-year swings — dropping as low as single digits in some years — a direct reflection of conflict destroying whatever health systems existed.

The global average (dashed line) has crept upward, but at a pace that will not close the gap for a generation without dramatic acceleration.

Conclusion

The data tells a clear and deeply uncomfortable story.

Where a child is born determines whether they will receive care when they are sick. A child in Chad or Somalia is not biologically less deserving of treatment than a child in Argentina or Cuba — they are simply born on the wrong side of a gap that politics and economics have created and continue to maintain.

Key findings from this analysis:

  • The global average ARI care rate has improved since 2000, but the gap between the best and worst performers has not meaningfully narrowed.
  • GDP per capita is positively correlated with care rates, but is not destiny — political investment in health systems matters independently of wealth.
  • Sub-Saharan Africa remains the most underserved region, with multiple countries below 30% for decades despite international attention.
  • Bangladesh and India prove that rapid progress is possible with sustained commitment.

UNICEF’s mandate demands action. Closing this gap requires investment in community health workers, supply chains for medicine, free-at-point-of-use care, and above all the political will to treat a child’s life in Chad as worth as much as a child’s life anywhere else.

“The true measure of a nation’s standing is how well it attends to its children.” — UNICEF

Data source: UNICEF Data Warehouse. Analysis conducted in Python using Polars and plotnine.
